title: Aluminum-Coated Austenitic Stainless Steel
subtitle: REU — Cal Poly Pomona & Mt. San Antonio College
venue: Cal Poly Pomona REU (Research Experience for Undergraduates)
advisor: Cal Poly Pomona Materials Engineering Lab
imageAlt: Stainless steel sample preparation for coating analysis
summary: As part of a summer REU program between Cal Poly Pomona and Mt. San Antonio College, I analyzed aluminum-coated austenitic stainless steel samples to determine the relationship between aluminum coating thickness and the nickel weight percent across different stainless steel grades.
description:
As a part of a summer REU (Research Experience for Undergraduates) program bridging Cal Poly Pomona and Mt. San Antonio College, I joined a research group focused on materials engineering. My specific task was to analyze substrate aluminum-coated austenitic stainless steel samples — austenitic being a classification of stainless steel defined by its specific chromium and nickel weight percentages.

The core research question was whether a measurable relationship existed between the thickness of the aluminum coating and the wt% of nickel in different stainless steel grades. Three grades were examined: SS 330 (Ni: 34.0–37.0%), SS 347 (Ni: 9.0–13.0%), and SS 302B (Ni: 8.0–10.0%).

Sample preparation followed standard metallographic procedures: each sample was ground through five progressively finer grits of silicon carbide paper — 240, 360, 600, 800, and 1200 grit. After polishing, samples were examined under a metallographic microscope and photographed. Coating thickness measurements were taken from the micrograph images using Fiji/ImageJ software.

The results indicated that wt% nickel is inversely proportional to aluminum coating thickness — that is, higher nickel content corresponds to thinner aluminum coatings. Findings were presented to a group of undergraduate researchers at the end of the program.
highlights: Determined that wt% nickel is inversely proportional to aluminum coating thickness across three SS grades
Prepared metallographic samples through five grits of SiC paper (240–1200 grit) using standard polishing procedures
Measured coating thickness from optical micrographs using Fiji/ImageJ image analysis software
Presented findings to a cohort of undergraduate researchers at Cal Poly Pomona
Gained hands-on experience in metallography, metal etching, and microscopy
